page_supplement.content: Okay, so let's get started. of your data.

bullets.ppt_bullet: Practical aspects of training / validation
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduces practical considerations for training and validation, including how the train/validation/test split ratios can vary drastically depending on dataset size. For very large datasets (e.g., 1 million samples), it may be sufficient to allocate as little as 1–5% for validation and testing, rather than the traditional 30%.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: So the percentage of training validation and testing could be very extreme. It could be 98% for training and 1% for validation and test. Or even if you have really 1 million data, then I will only leave 5,000 for validation.

ppt_text: Predict Who Will Repay TheirLoans •A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans •You are given complete files on 100 •5 defaulted within 3 years Image creditdebt.org

bullets.ppt_bullet: Predict Who Will Repay Their Loans
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduces the problem as predicting which loan applicants will repay and which will default. This is framed as a practical machine learning task for a lender.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: I may want to predict who will repay their loan and who will default.

bullets.ppt_bullet: A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their loans
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher describes the scenario where a lender brings you in to investigate the repayment problem, giving you access to applicant files.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: a lender helps you to investigate this problem and you are given the files of 100 applicants.

bullets.ppt_bullet: You are given complete files on 100
bullets.ai_comment: The dataset consists of complete files for 100 loan applicants, which serves as the training data for the model.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: you are given the files of 100 applicants.

bullets.ppt_bullet: 5 defaulted within 3 years
bullets.ai_comment: Out of the 100 applicants, exactly 5 defaulted within three years, and the goal is to build a model to predict who else might default.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: Among these 100 applicants, there are five of them defaulted within three years. So your goal is to build a model and predict who else is going to default.

ppt_text:
A Surprising Finding
• All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
• Your model may find this strong signal as well

page_supplement.content: So whether this is a valid feature or whether there are some problem when we make this conclusion. have no codal inference with whether he's going to default or not. So because of that, we know that this feature may not be a valid feature.

bullets.ppt_bullet: A Surprising Finding
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduces a counterintuitive example to illustrate a common modeling pitfall. This sets up a case where a seemingly strong signal turns out to be meaningless.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: For such a job, what you may find is they give you some photos and they find that all the five people who defaulted worn blue shirts during their interviews.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher explains that in the dataset, every one of the five defaulters happened to wear a blue shirt, making it appear as a pattern. However, this is not a valid causal signal since the sample is too small and the overlap is coincidental.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: All the five people who defaulted worn blue shirts during their interviews. This is a very classic problem of overfitting or there's no codal evidence that blue shirts actually predict applicant's default behavior.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Your model may find this strong signal as well
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher warns that a machine learning model can pick up on this spurious correlation and treat blue shirts as a strong predictor of default. This illustrates how models can overfit to noise rather than true underlying patterns.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: And your model also find that blue shirts is a strong signal for default.

ppt_text:
Training Error and Generalization Error
• Training error (bias): model error on the training data
• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
• Example: practice a future exam with past exams
• Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn’t guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
• Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers

page_supplement.content: That means he remember all the answers mechanically. as long as he can understand the reasonings, he should be able to have a higher score. or they call it bias and virus in some cases, right?

bullets.ppt_bullet: Training Error and Generalization Error
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduced this as a classic discussion in machine learning. It serves as the overarching topic for the slide.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: And this introduced our classic discussion of training error and generalization error.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Training error (bias): model error on the training data
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher defined training error as the error measured on the training dataset itself. This corresponds to the 'bias' term in the bias-variance trade-off.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: training error means the error on the training data set.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
bullets.ai_comment: Generalization error was defined as the error the model makes on new, unseen data. This is distinct from training error and reflects real-world performance.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: And generalization error refers to your error on a new data.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Example: practice a future exam with past exams
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher used the analogy of studying for a future exam using past exams to make the concept intuitive. Past exams represent training data, and the future exam represents new data.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: This is very similar to we make practices for a future exam with past exams.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn't guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher emphasized that low training error (doing well on past exams) does not guarantee low generalization error (a good score on the future exam). The future exam explicitly represents generalization error in this analogy.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: For those who do well on past exam, that means you get a very small training error that does not guarantee a good score on the future exam, where the future exam actually represent the generalization error, right?

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher described Student A as someone who achieves zero error on past exams through rote learning, but implied this approach fails to generalize. This illustrates overfitting to training data.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: And one student may get zero error on past exam by root learning.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers
bullets.ai_comment: Student B understands the reasoning behind the answers and therefore generalizes better to the future exam, even if their past-exam scores are not perfect. This illustrates better generalization performance.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: But that student may have a worse score compared to another student who understand the reasons... So even if student B may not have good score on past exam, may not that good at past exam.

ppt_text:
Validation Dataset and TestDataset
• Validation dataset: a dataset used to evaluate themodel
• Should not be mixed with the training data (#1 mistake)
• Model selection purpose •Test dataset: a dataset can be used once, e.g.
• A future exam
• The house sale price I bided
• Dataset used in private leaderboard in Kaggle

page_supplement.content: So that could be a future exam or the health cell price I bid it or the data set we use

bullets.ppt_bullet: Validation Dataset and TestDataset
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduced two key dataset types: the validation dataset and the test dataset, each serving a distinct purpose in the model development pipeline.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: One data set is called validation data set.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Validation dataset: a dataset used to evaluate the model
bullets.ai_comment: The validation dataset is used for model selection — deciding which model to go with — and all model selection criteria should be based on performance measured on it.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: And the validation data set will be used to do model selection, which model should... Your model selection criteria is the performance on the validation data set.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Should not be mixed with the training data (#1 mistake)
bullets.ai_comment: (not covered in lecture)

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Model selection purpose •Test dataset: a dataset can be used once, e.g.
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher clarified that the test dataset is reserved to evaluate final performance and can only be used once, distinguishing it clearly from the validation set.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: The test data set will be the data set that to evaluate your final performance.

bullets.ppt_bullet: • A future exam
bullets.ai_comment: (not covered in lecture)

bullets.ppt_bullet: • The house sale price I bided
bullets.ai_comment: (not covered in lecture)

bullets.ppt_bullet: • Dataset used in private leaderboard in Kaggle
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher explained that in Kaggle competitions, the private leaderboard uses a hidden test dataset to determine the final winner, and homework 2 will include a similar test set.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: to determine the final winner on a Kaggle computation that is called private leaderboard. So in your homework 2, you will actually have such a test data set.

ppt_text:
• Previous ML: 70/30
• Big data: 1,000,000 10,000 10,000
• Percentages: 98/1/1
• Percentages: 99.5/0.4/0.1
• K-fold CV is expensive for deep learning

page_supplement.content: This is the most classic way. because now I have a very large data.

bullets.ppt_bullet: Previous ML: 70/30
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher explains that the classic 70/30 train/test split learned in previous ML courses is not a good idea for deep learning. This is because deep learning datasets are much larger, making a 70% training split unnecessarily large.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: you may have learned I should split the data 70-30. However, all of them are not applicable for deep learning models... splitting the data in a 70-30 proportion may also not a good idea

bullets.ai_comment: The teacher notes that in deep learning, datasets can reach 1 million or even 1 billion data points, so it is sufficient to reserve only around 10,000 examples each for the dev and test sets.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: I may have 1 million or even 1 billion data points... for deep learning, people usually leave 10,000 or 1,000 data points

bullets.ppt_bullet: Percentages: 98/1/1
bullets.ai_comment: (not covered in lecture)

bullets.ppt_bullet: Percentages: 99.5/0.4/0.1
bullets.ai_comment: (not covered in lecture)

bullets.ppt_bullet: K-fold CV is expensive for deep learning
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher explicitly states that k-fold cross-validation is too expensive for deep learning because training a deep learning model is already very costly, making it impractical to repeat k times.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: k-fold cross-validation is too expensive because you have known that training a deep... using k-fold.

ppt_text: Basic recipe for machinelearning •High bias (training data performance) → Underfitting → •High variance (validation set performance) → Overfitting →

bullets.ppt_bullet: Basic recipe for machine learning • High bias (training data performance) → Underfitting → • High variance (validation set performance) → Overfitting →
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher noted that training data performance alone does not represent true model performance, implying the need to also evaluate on a validation set to detect overfitting or underfitting. This recipe helps diagnose whether a model suffers from high bias (underfitting) or high variance (overfitting).
bullets.transcript_excerpt: the performance on the training data does not represent the performance... this is a difference between traditional machine learning model training as well as

ppt_text:
•Model capacity and data capacity should be matched to prevent overfitting and underfitting.
•Understand fitting and bias/variance relationship •Empirically, use training/validation error to evaluate •Know what to do under whichcircumstance

bullets.ppt_bullet: Model capacity and data capacity should be matched to prevent overfitting and underfitting.
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher emphasized the need to evaluate the model to determine if it fits well, implying that matching model and data capacity is key to avoiding poor generalization. Keeping validation data separate from training data is part of this process.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: we need to evaluate our model to understand whether it has a good... And this data should not be mixed with your training data.

bullets.ppt_bullet: Understand fitting and bias/variance relationship •Empirically, use training/validation error to evaluate •Know what to do under which circumstance
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher discussed using training and validation splits empirically to evaluate model performance, noting that the vast majority of data (99.5%) can be used for training while reserving a portion for testing.
bullets.transcript_excerpt: we need to evaluate our model to understand whether it has a good... use all the 99.5% of the data to train the model.
